roth.note: Alabama exempts defined-benefit PENSIONS (government, military, many private) and Social Security — but traditional IRA and 401(k) distributions are FULLY TAXABLE at up to 5%. A Roth conversion is IRA income, so Alabama taxes it; those 65+ can exclude only the first $6,000. The tool treats the conversion as taxable, which is correct here.
No state income tax.

Flat 2.5% (one of the lowest in the nation), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. The flat rate means a conversion is taxed at 2.5% regardless of size, making Arizona one of the cheaper states for a large conversion.

Top rate 3.9% (2026), reached at low income, so a conversion is effectively taxed near 3.9%. A $6,000 retirement exemption covers traditional IRA income but only at 59.5+ (early distributions do not qualify), and it is a small cap a conversion exceeds. Social Security is exempt below $100k AGI; a large conversion could push past that.

No retirement income exemption — IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxed as regular income through nine progressive brackets (1% to 12.3%), plus a 1% surcharge on income over $1M (13.3% top). The 9.3% bracket used here covers roughly $73k-$371k single income, where most filers land; your rate may be lower or higher. Social Security is not taxed by California.

Flat 4.4% (sometimes reduced by TABOR refunds). A pension/annuity subtraction covers IRA and conversion income — up to $20k at 55-64 and $24k at 65+ — but it specifically EXCLUDES early (pre-59.5) distributions, and a meaningful conversion exceeds the cap. Colorado fully exempts Social Security at 65+.

For 2026 Connecticut fully exempts IRA, 401(k), and pension income ONLY if AGI stays under $75,000 single / $100,000 joint, phasing out to nothing at a $100,000 / $150,000 cliff. A Roth conversion raises AGI and a sizable one can blow past the cliff, making it taxable (graduated 2% to 6.99%; about 5.5% is typical). This tool assumes the conversion is taxable; a small one that keeps you under the threshold may not be. Connecticut also taxes Social Security above those same thresholds.

Graduated 2.2% to 6.6%; the 6.6% top only starts at $60k, so a typical conversion lands in the 5.55% band used here. At 60+, a $12,500 exclusion covers combined pension + IRA income, but it is a small cap a meaningful conversion exceeds — and an early distribution (before 59.5) does not qualify at all. Social Security is exempt and there is no sales tax.
No state income tax.

Georgia excludes retirement income up to a CAP — $65,000 per person at 65+ ($35,000 at 62-64) — then taxes the rest at its flat rate (about 5.39% for 2026, falling on a trigger schedule). A Roth conversion is large income that can exceed the cap, so the excess is taxable; this tool assumes the conversion is taxable, though a small conversion that fits under your exclusion may not be. Social Security is fully exempt and does not use up the cap.

Graduated 1.4% to 11% across twelve brackets; the 11% top only applies above $200k single / $400k joint, and a 2024 reform (Act 46) widened the brackets, so a typical conversion lands near 8% (used here) and the rate keeps falling through 2031. Hawaii fully exempts pensions and Social Security, but traditional IRA and 401(k) income is fully taxable — a Roth conversion is IRA income, so Hawaii taxes it.

Flat 5.3% (cut from 5.695% under 2025 reform, retroactive to that year), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. Being flat, the rate on a conversion does not change with its size.

Illinois exempts qualified retirement income — IRA, 401(k), and pension — from its 4.95% flat tax, and the Department of Revenue treats the taxable portion of a Roth conversion as exempt too (subtracted on Schedule M). No age requirement, no income cap, no cliff — so a conversion is genuinely Illinois-tax-free. Social Security is also exempt.

Flat 2.95% for 2026 (cut from 3.05%, scheduled to keep falling toward ~2.9% in 2027), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. The flat rate means a conversion is taxed the same regardless of size; some counties add a small local income tax on top.

Since 2023 Iowa fully exempts retirement income — including Roth conversion income — for residents age 55 and older, so a conversion at 55+ is Iowa-tax-free. Under 55, the conversion is taxable at Iowa's flat rate (about 3.9% for 2026, still falling). Social Security is fully exempt at any age.

Two brackets; the top ~5.58% rate starts at just ~$23k single, so a typical conversion is taxed near that rate. IRA, 401(k), and conversion income is fully taxable (KPERS, federal, and military pensions are exempt, but private retirement accounts are not). Kansas fully exempted Social Security starting in 2024, so a conversion here does not pull benefits into tax.

Flat 3.5% (cut from 4% on Jan 1 2026 via a revenue trigger), applied to IRA and conversion income. Kentucky exempts Social Security and gives a modest retirement exclusion, but a meaningful conversion is taxable beyond it.
Flat 4.25% (2026); IRA and conversion income is taxable, with a $6,000 retirement-income exclusion per person at 65+. A conversion above that is fully taxable.

Graduated 5.8% to 7.15%; the 7.15% top starts near $65k single / $130k joint, so a typical conversion lands in the 6.75% band used here. Important: Maine's pension income deduction does NOT apply to Roth conversions — they are specifically excluded — so the conversion is fully taxable with no shelter. Social Security is exempt.

State 2% to 5.75% (plus new 6.25% and 6.5% brackets above ~$250k), AND a mandatory county tax of about 2.25% to 3.2% on top — so the real rate is higher than the state figure shown. Important: Maryland's pension exclusion (about $36k at 65+) covers 401(k)/403(b)/pension income but EXCLUDES all IRAs, so a Roth conversion gets no shelter and is fully taxable. Social Security is exempt.

Flat 5% on most income including IRA and conversion income, with NO retirement exclusion (Massachusetts taxes IRA/401(k) withdrawals in full). Watch the 4% surtax on income above $1M: a very large conversion could push that year past the threshold and add 4% on the excess. Social Security is exempt.

Flat 4.25%. For 2026 the Lowering MI Costs phase-in is complete: retirees can deduct retirement and pension income (including IRA withdrawals) up to about $67,610 single / $135,220 joint, regardless of birth year. Social Security is fully exempt from Michigan tax. A Roth conversion is taxable IRA income, so whether it falls under the deduction cap depends on your other retirement income — verify for your situation.

Graduated 5.35% to 9.85%; the 9.85% top only starts near $193k single / $305k joint, so a typical conversion lands in the 7.85% band used here. IRA and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable (no broad retirement exclusion). Watch a second effect: a conversion raises your income and can shrink Minnesota's Social Security subtraction, pulling more of your benefits into MN tax that year.

Mississippi exempts qualified retirement income — IRA, 401(k), and pension — once you have met the plan's retirement requirements (generally age 59.5). A conversion at 59.5+ is therefore MS-tax-free; an EARLY distribution before then does not qualify and is taxed at the flat rate (4.4% for 2026, stepping down toward 3.75%). Social Security is fully exempt.

Graduated 2% to 4.7%, but the 4.7% top rate starts at just ~$9,200 of taxable income, so a conversion is effectively taxed at 4.7%. IRA and conversion income is taxable; the only applicable break is a $6,000 private-pension exclusion that phases out under $25k single / $32k joint, so a meaningful conversion rarely qualifies. Social Security is fully exempt (62+).

Two brackets (4.7% / 5.65% for 2026, top rate falling to 5.4% in 2027); the 5.65% rate starts at just ~$20,500 single / ~$41,000 joint, so most conversion income is taxed at 5.65%. IRA and conversion income is taxable; Montana repealed its broader retirement deduction and now allows only a small (~$5,500) qualified-retirement-income deduction. Montana also taxes Social Security on the federal formula, so a conversion can pull more of your benefits into tax. No sales tax.

Top rate is 4.55% for 2026 (down from 5.84%; falling to 3.99% in 2027 under LB 754), and a typical conversion lands at or near that top rate. IRA and conversion income is fully taxable with no special exemption. Social Security is now fully exempt (as of 2025) regardless of income — so unlike some neighbors, a conversion here does not pull more SS into tax.
No state income tax.
No income tax (interest/dividend tax ended 2025).

New Jersey fully excludes retirement income (including IRA withdrawals) for those 62+ ONLY if total income stays at or below $100,000, phasing out to nothing at a hard $150,000 cliff. A Roth conversion counts toward that income, so a sizable one can push you over the cliff and make the whole conversion taxable at rates up to 10.75% (6.37% is typical). This tool assumes the conversion is taxable; if your total income stays under the threshold it may not be. Social Security is fully exempt.

Graduated 1.5% to 5.9%; the 5.9% top only applies above $210k single / $315k joint, so a typical conversion lands near the 4.9% band used here. IRA and conversion income is taxable; those 65+ can deduct up to $8,000, but only under $100k single / $150k joint AGI. A conversion raises AGI and can blow past that limit — and past the same $100k/$150k threshold above which New Mexico starts taxing Social Security too.

Graduated 4% to 10.9% (the 10.9% top only applies above $25M; 6.25% here reflects a typical conversion). IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable, but those 59.5+ can exclude $20,000 of private retirement income (per person; still $20,000 for 2026 — proposed increases were not enacted). Government pensions and Social Security are fully exempt. NYC residents also pay city tax up to ~3.876%.

Flat 3.99% for 2026 (final step of a phasedown; scheduled to keep falling toward 2.99% by 2028), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. Being flat, the conversion is taxed at the same rate regardless of size.

Very low rates topping out at 2.5%, applied to IRA and conversion income — among the lowest state taxes on a conversion anywhere. Social Security is exempt. North Dakota has a 0% bottom bracket, so part of a small conversion may not be taxed at all.

For 2026 Ohio uses a flat 2.75% on income above $26,050 (0% below). IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable at that rate; a small retirement-income credit (up to $200 total) barely offsets a conversion. Social Security is fully exempt from Ohio tax.

Restructured for 2026: top rate cut to 4.5%, reached at low income, so a conversion is effectively taxed at 4.5%. The $10,000 retirement exclusion (65+) does include IRA income, but it is a small cap a meaningful conversion exceeds, leaving the rest taxable. Social Security is fully exempt.

Graduated 4.75% to 9.9%; the 8.75% bracket runs up to $125,000, so most conversion income lands there (9.9% applies above that). IRA and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable, and the retirement-income credit is limited to low household income, so it rarely helps with a conversion. Social Security is fully exempt; Oregon has no sales tax. Portland-area residents may owe extra county/metro income tax.

Pennsylvania exempts retirement income — IRA, 401(k), and pension distributions — from its flat 3.07% tax once you reach retirement age (59.5 for IRAs). A Roth conversion at 59.5+ is therefore PA-tax-free; a conversion before 59.5 could be taxed as an early distribution. Social Security is fully exempt. (PA also taxes 401(k)/IRA contributions up front, so there is no further state tax at withdrawal.)

Three brackets (3.75% / 4.75% / 5.99%); 5.99% only starts near $176k, so a typical conversion lands in the 4.75% band used here. Important: Rhode Island's up-to-$50,000 retirement-income modification covers 401(k)/403(b)/pension/annuity income but specifically EXCLUDES all IRAs — so a Roth conversion gets no shelter and is fully taxable. Rhode Island also taxes Social Security above ~$107k single / ~$133.75k joint, a threshold a conversion can cross.

Rate cut sharply under 2026 reform (top about 5.2%, down from 7%, with further cuts tied to revenue growth). Unlike many states, the retirement deduction DOES cover traditional IRA income; those 65+ also get a separate $15,000 deduction against any income that can absorb conversion income — so post-65 is a relatively favorable conversion window here. Social Security is fully exempt.
No state income tax.
No income tax.
No state income tax.

Flat 4.5% (recently cut), applied to IRA and conversion income. Utah is one of the few states that taxes Social Security, though a phased credit offsets it for lower incomes; the flat structure means a conversion is taxed at the same rate at any size. A small retirement credit applies but phases out as income rises.

Graduated 3.35% to 8.75%; the 8.75% top only starts near $230k single / $280k joint, so a typical conversion lands in the 6.6% band used here. IRA and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable (only a small $10k deduction for certain pension/government retirement income, income-limited). A conversion can also push you past Vermont's Social Security exemption thresholds, taxing more of your benefits that year.

Top rate 5.75% starts at just $17,000, so essentially all conversion income is taxed at 5.75%. IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable. Those 65+ get a $12,000 age deduction, but it phases out dollar-for-dollar above $50,000 single / $75,000 joint AGI — and a conversion raises your AGI, so a large one can erase the deduction. Social Security is fully exempt.

No income tax, so IRA and Roth-conversion income is untaxed (the 7% capital-gains tax exempts retirement accounts). One thing to watch: a new state income tax of 9.9% on household income above $1 million takes effect in 2028 and counts conversion income toward that threshold — so only a very large conversion in 2028 or later could be affected. Social Security is exempt.

Top rate about 4.86% after a 5% cut retroactive to Jan 1 2026 (West Virginia is cutting income tax most years, so this keeps falling). IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable; seniors 65+ get an $8,000 modification. Social Security is now fully exempt for all residents (phase-out completed in 2026).

Graduated 3.5% to 7.65%; the 7.65% top only starts above ~$315k single / ~$420k joint, so a typical conversion lands in the 5.3% band used here. IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable, but a 2025 law (Act 15) lets those 67+ subtract up to $24,000 ($48,000 joint) of retirement income — including IRA/conversion income — so part of a conversion may be sheltered. Social Security is fully exempt.
No state income tax.

Graduated 4% to 10.75%; the 10.75% top only applies above $1M, while the 8.5% band covers roughly $60k-$250k where most conversion income lands (used here). IRA, 401(k), and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable; there is no retirement-income exclusion. Social Security is exempt.
